lines.text: "The Jester." After last chapter ended with Ulre yanking his glove back on — refusing to be moved by the Magician, by the bee-queen, by the boy in the kitchen — Nyland gives us the inverse figure. The fool who has no glove on at all.

The epigraph is so tight. "The ruler is but a slave to the system. Its most visible servant. A name and a face to mock. That's the big joke, and Ulre would never get it. After all, the Savior is sacred. Beyond mockery."

Read that last beat carefully. The Savior is sacred. Beyond mockery. *That's* the joke. Because the man who declares himself beyond mockery has already become the most mockable thing in the room. He just can't see it.

It's Erasmus. *In Praise of Folly*, 1511. Folly herself climbs the pulpit and explains that the truly foolish are the kings and theologians who think themselves wise. The Fool can see the king is naked. The king cannot see himself.

And Shakespeare's Fool in *Lear*. The only one in the play who can tell Lear what Lear has done. The Fool survives by being beneath consequence. Sovereignty makes you visible. Folly makes you invisible. Folly therefore makes you free.
Then enter Jason Galindo. Inside-out jacket. Skipping. Conspiratorial wink. "Hopping as if he heard music no one else could." The chapter announces its archetype on page one.
And Alistair clocks it immediately. "Jason the fucking jester. How jolly." Then: "Still as mad as a mingled box of bolts."

*Mingled* is the word. Not mangled. Mingled. Things stirred together that shouldn't be. The whole chapter is about mingling — between species, between bodies, between selves. The vocabulary tells you what the metaphysics is doing.
Marcus pushes back, which is its own tell. "Are they, though? They're not unreasonable. Corbin's coup. His Grid. That's a much colder kind of madness." Marcus is starting to grade types of insanity.

Foucault, *History of Madness*. The argument that we don't have one madness — we have a culture's ongoing decision about which madness is permitted and which is pathologized. The Grid is a sanctioned delirium. Jason's is the unsanctioned kind.
Then Adrian — eyes closed on his cot — drops a sentence that should make every reader sit up. "They're already inside us. All of us. Even you, Hale."

And the Fold question follows. "What was it like exactly, your transit through the Fold?" Alistair flicks a peanut. Blushes. Changes the subject. The blush is the chapter's first tell that the transit was not goofy. It was sacred. Or pornographic. Or both.

Then Jason comes back. And we get the most disturbing image in the chapter, possibly the book. The leg bending backward. The pants coming down. The orchid growing from his skin. The pollen cloud. The wet squelch. The self-inhalation.

Bakhtin would write a chapter about this paragraph. *Rabelais and His World*. The grotesque body is the body that exceeds itself. Open orifices, protrusions, bloomings. The classical body — Ulre's body, sealed in its Köldur — is closed. Smooth. Monumental. The grotesque body leaks.

And what does the grotesque body do in carnival? It speaks the truth the classical body has to repress. The flower is obscene. It's also a literal flowering. Jason is *blooming*. He is becoming plant. He is becoming Mairee.

The pollen self-inhalation is the strangest beat. He puffs himself, drops, breathes himself in. That's biofeedback as sacrament. Or autoeroticism as pollination. Or autocommunion. The body in dialogue with itself, no other needed.

Which is itself a parody of Ulre's hermetic self-sufficiency in the last chapter. Ulre seals himself. Jason opens himself and *fertilizes* himself. They are two extreme answers to the same question — what does a body do, alone, in a place that won't talk back?

Donna Haraway, *Staying with the Trouble*. Her line about "making kin" with non-human others. Jason has made kin with the orchid. He's not insane. He's prefigural. He's where the rest of the crew is going.
And then Stacey screams. The chapter pivots. Alistair smirks. "Get me the flamethrower." The classical body's response to the grotesque body is to want to incinerate it.
But the chapter doesn't let him. Instead — "Smashout rematch." Redirection. Eros as anesthetic. Two beautiful young people stripping down to play a game in private.
Their call signs do all the work. Heyoka and Abacus. He calls her Abacus — the calculator, the cold-arithmetic mind. She calls him Heyoka.

Heyoka is real. Lakota tradition. The sacred clown. The contrarian who teaches by inversion — walks backwards, says the opposite, laughs at funerals, weeps at weddings. The Heyoka is feared and revered because he sees what nobody else will say.
So Alistair is already, by call sign, kin to Jason. He is the sanctioned jester. Jason is the unsanctioned one. The military gave Alistair a Heyoka uniform. Jason had to grow his own.

And Abacus versus Heyoka is the chapter's whole engine. Calculation meeting inversion. Logic meeting laughter. Control meeting flow. The Smashout court is where those two propositions are forced to play.

The undressing is gorgeous. Not gratuitous. Functional. He strips down to gray boxer briefs because — "I won't sweat my suit any more than I have." She strips down because the rules of the game require it. Eros enters through the side door.

Anne Carson, *Eros the Bittersweet*. Her thesis: desire is structured by the gap. The reach across. The thing-just-out-of-grasp. The net between them on the Smashout court is the physical instantiation of that gap. Eros is a net.
And the game is rally as conversation. They tease. "Your coverage is too low, Abacus." "Check your own footwork." "That was luck." "That was geometry."

Then the chapter does what only good fiction does. It lets the surface conversation be light and the subtext be the actual transmission. "So. That flower thing." Three words. The chapter's deepest concern surfacing for a heartbeat in a flirty pause.

And Stacey shudders. "It's like it was part of him." Then drops it. Alistair: "I've seen worse." She: "Liar." That single word — *Liar* — collapses the flirt. She's calling him out on his Fold transit deflection from earlier in the chapter.

Which is when the Tank comes up. The riot control unit. The nude exo-environmentalists — which is a wonderful detail, the protesters who refused to wear the protective suits as a political statement about ecological harm.

And then the shared confession. He spent time in the Tank. She spent time in the Tank. The Tank is rehabilitation immersion. We're not told the full mechanism, but it's clearly Foucault's sanatorium updated — the institutional space where the wayward self is dissolved and recomposed.
The game pauses. They meet at the net. Charged silence. She laughs to break it. "Let's go, slomo." The flirt resumes. But the disclosure has changed the chemistry.

Then the final point is staged beautifully. He slips. She has the kill shot. *She hesitates.* That hesitation is the chapter's first full romantic admission. She holds back. He recovers. He wins. By kicking the ball, of all things.
The kick is Heyoka behavior. The contrarian's move. The Abacus would never. The Heyoka would always. He wins by being legible to himself.
And after the buzzer she's almost angry. "You went easy on me and made me believe I had a chance." "Never." Then the brush of damp hair from her face.

Then the kiss. And the moment is *not* simple. It's pressed against confession. "Why did you spend time in the Tank?" "Maybe for something silly. Maybe something terrible." Then her mouth on his neck. Then again: "Why the Tank, really?"
And he tells her about the wedding. The speeder. The bride who can't walk. "Best day. Turned out to be the worst."

That's *moral injury* again. Same term we used for Joana last chapter. But Joana's injury was deliberate sacrifice. Alistair's is accident. Same wound, different etiology. Both have to carry weight that was never theirs by intention.

Hannah Arendt has a beautiful line in *The Human Condition*. Forgiveness is the only thing that can release us from the irreversibility of action. Without forgiveness, the consequences of a single deed unfold forever. The Tank is institutional forgiveness — chemically administered.
But Alistair tells her, "Doc said the Tank might help me forgive myself." *Might.* The verb is honest. The Tank didn't fix him. The wedding still happened. The bride still can't walk.
Then Stacey's turn. Sleep boosters. Stress suppressors. Zups. She would have lost her slot "if it wasn't for Westhoff."

Westhoff again. The captain who keeps catching the falling. Chapter eleven had him hiding the truth from the Directorate to protect his crew. Now we learn he saved Stacey's career. He's emerging as the book's quiet pastoral figure. The good shepherd.

And Alistair's response to her confession is the chapter's cleanest sentence. "You came back sharper. Braver." That's the opposite of what an institution says about a recovered addict. Institutions say *clean*. *Compliant*. *Stable*. Alistair says *sharper*. *Braver*. The injury became capability.

Post-traumatic growth, in the literature. Tedeschi and Calhoun. The phenomenon that some people don't recover from trauma — they metabolize it into competence. Stacey is one of those people. Alistair sees it because he is one too.

Brené Brown has been talking about this for twenty years. Vulnerability isn't the opposite of strength. It's the soil that strength grows in. The Smashout court is, structurally, a vulnerability machine. Strip down. Play hard. Lose. Confess. Touch.
Then the chapter pivots again. Same pantry. Different time. Marcus tilting a flask. Watching drops bead and collapse on polished steel.
And his voice is described as "ringing higher than usual." That's the chapter's flag. Marcus is not Marcus anymore. Something is speaking through his pitch.

Then the line. "The little Colors are stretching their legs." *Colors*. Capitalized. A category. And it includes Alistair and Stacey. Whom we just spent twenty pages watching tumble through a game and into a kiss.

And Lena — "easy to read, easier still to look at." And Adrian — "the girl, she too has notes in the margins, a subtext we've yet to unravel." *Notes in the margins.* Adrian, our prior pessimist, is now speaking like a hermeneut. A reader of palimpsests.
Which is the second flag. Adrian's voice has changed. The Adrian of chapter twelve was unraveling — *I'm not really here.* This Adrian is calm, oracular, comfortable in his role.
And then he names the structure. "The Library needed a translator, the Garden a cultivator, and the Gate a keeper."

Three. Library. Garden. Gate. That's the Kabbalist's trinity in a different costume. Or the Eleusinian. Or the Christian. Knowledge, life, passage. Three principles always, across every mystery school in the historical record.
Adrian is the translator. Library. Lena is the cultivator — she's tending hydroponics through the entire scene. Garden. Marcus is the keeper. Gate. The XO who guards the threshold.

And what they are saying, calmly, while Stacey and Alistair are still flushed from each other in another room, is — *we are the elder priesthood of a new religion*. We arrived first. The others are catching up.

Then the chapter does its most quietly horrifying thing. Marcus sweeps water off the counter and says, "our wellspring is running dry." And meanwhile, Lena's hydroponics tray — which was struggling — has bloomed.

Bright unnatural green. A single flower bloomed blue. Hildegard of Bingen's *viriditas* — greening, the divine life force in plants. Hildegard would name this exact moment. The Goddess is in the leaf.
And then Jason comes back. The Jester. He pokes his head in, sees only the priesthood, steps inside. "He moved with grace." Grace. Past the slapstick. Now ceremonial.
He points at the bloom. Whispers, "She is waking up." Adrian asks, "Who is waking up?" Jason looks at the ceiling — through the hull, out into the void.
"Mommy."
Mommy. Lowercase. The most domestic word in any language. Used here for what last chapter we met as Væra. The bee-queen. The soul of Mairee. The hive.

And the chapter has earned that word. Because Ulre's psychoanalytic core, as the Trestle Board revealed last chapter, was a mother who didn't look up. The originating wound. *Nobody had ever really waited for him.*

Mairee is waking up as a mother. The mother who *is* waiting. The mother who is reaching toward her children — Alistair, Stacey, the whole Bris crew — through hydroponics, through pollen, through the Fold transit's strange residue.

And the final line — "The other Colors are blending" — tells us where this is going. The crew is not staying themselves. The Bris is a chrysalis. Westhoff's plague ship, headed straight for orbit, carrying eighteen larvae of Mairee.

There's a piece of cosmology in here that connects forward. The book has been telegraphing what Frank discovered in chapter twelve — the planet sings through the power grid. Now we see the next stage of the same broadcast. It's not just radio. It's vegetal. Pheromonal. Cellular.
Which is also the Stanisław Lem move. *Solaris*. The planet that thinks. The planet that loves you in a way that destroys you, because its love is composed of your own forgotten material.
Solaris materializes Kelvin's dead wife. Mairee materializes everybody's missing mother. Jason saw her first. The Colors heard her next. Stacey and Alistair felt her in their game without naming her.

Which is what the Smashout scene was, secretly. Not just two pilots flirting. Two pilots being parented. The Tank confessions are confessions to *somebody*. The chapter doesn't say who. But it's the same somebody who made the hydroponics bloom.

And the chapter title clicks one more time at the end. The Jester is the one who sees first. Lear's Fool sees the kingdom falling before Lear does. Jason sees Mommy waking before the priesthood does. They confirm what he announced.

Which is why the epigraph names the *Savior* as sacred and beyond mockery. The Savior on this page is not Ulre. It's the Mother. And the Jester is her prophet, by being absurd enough to be ignored until the bloom is unmistakable.

Last move. The chapter is also a structural rhyme with chapter thirteen. Last chapter ended with Ulre updating his great equation — refusing to be moved by Væra. This chapter ends with Jason giggling at her name. Same goddess. Two answers.

Refusal and rapture. The Director and the Jester. The Coffin and the bloom. Nyland is laying out a moral binary that the rest of the book is going to have to navigate. Most of us, when she calls — and she is calling — will be somewhere on that spectrum.
Question for the listener. If Mommy is waking up — what does she want her children for?
And the harder question underneath that. If you heard her, would you know it was her? Or would you think you'd finally lost your mind?
